1: Carnegie Mellon University was founded in 1900 as Carnegie Technical Schools by Andrew Carnegie.
2: The original purpose of the Carnegie Technical Schools was to provide working-class men and women in Pittsburgh with practical skills, trades, and crafts to enhance their careers, lives, and communities. The schools offered two- and three-year certificates in arts and engineering disciplines, including a college for women.
4: In 1967, Carnegie Institute of Technology merged with the Mellon Institute of Industrial Research to form Carnegie Mellon University, supported by Paul Mellon.
5: The Mellon Institute of Industrial Research was founded in 1913 by Andrew Mellon and Richard B.
6: The motto of Carnegie Mellon University is "My heart is in the work.
7: Carnegie Mellon University is located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, United States
8: Carnegie Mellon University comprises seven colleges and schools. These include the College of Engineering, the College of Fine Arts, the Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences, the Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy, the Mellon College of Science, the School of Computer Science, and the Tepper School of Business.
9: The seven colleges and schools at Carnegie Mellon University are the College of Engineering, College of Fine Arts, Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences, Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy, Mellon College of Science, School of Computer Science, and Tepper School of Business.
10: The School of Computer Science at Carnegie Mellon University is renowned for its computer science program. This school offers extensive coursework in areas such as mathematics, programming, and computer systems, and is known for its interdisciplinary approach and cutting-edge research.
11: The name of Carnegie Mellon's business school is the Tepper School of Business, originally known as the Graduate School of Industrial Administration (GSIA). It was renamed in 2004 after receiving a $55 million gift from alumnus David Tepper.
12: Mel Bochner is a notable artist and alumnus of Carnegie Mellon University associated with the Pop Art movement. He was a pioneering figure in the development of conceptual art.
13: The name of Carnegie Mellon's student newspaper is The Tartan
14: The official mascot of Carnegie Mellon University is a Scottish terrier named Scotty.
15: The official colors of Carnegie Mellon University are cardinal and gray
16: The annual event where Carnegie Mellon students build and race buggies is called Sweepstakes, which is a highlight of Spring Carnival.
17: The Fence at Carnegie Mellon University is a concrete and steel structure located in the middle of the Cut, dating back to 1923. It is frequently painted with witty messages, advertisements, and accomplishments, serving as a prominent canvas for student expression.
18: The College of Fine Arts is housed in a building designed by Henry Hornbostel, located in the Oakland District of Pittsburgh. This facility includes schools of architecture, art, and music, as well as several BXA intercollege degree programs.
19: The main library at Carnegie Mellon University is Hunt Library
20: Randy Pausch, a professor of computer science, human-computer interaction
21: Randy Pausch's "Last Lecture," given on September 18, 2007, at Carnegie Mellon, became known worldwide for its inspirational message on achieving childhood dreams and leaving a positive legacy despite his terminal diagnosis. The lecture was later adapted into a bestselling book that topped bestseller lists across the country.
22: Andy Bechtolsheim co-founded Sun Microsystems. He earned his M.S
23: Carnegie Mellon's annual spring carnival is simply called Spring Carnival
24: The distinctive cylindrical building on Carnegie Mellon University's campus is the University Center, which replaced the original Skibo Hall in 1996. This building features a unique cylindrical design and was renamed the Cohon University Center in 2014.
25: The Kiltie Band is the concert and marching band of Carnegie Mellon University, known for wearing kilts in honor of Andrew Carnegie's Scottish-American heritage. It supports various sports teams, performs at home football games, and holds concerts during the holiday and spring seasons.
26: The Robotics Institute at Carnegie Mellon University focuses on robotics research. Established in 1979, it is a division of the School of Computer Science and is recognized as one of the world's leading robotics research facilities.
27: The interdisciplinary program combining technology and the arts at Carnegie Mellon University is called IDeATe, which stands for Integrative Design, Arts, and Technology. This program offers minors and courses that are open to all majors and covers areas such as game design, animation, media design, and sonic arts.
28: Scott Fahlman, a Carnegie Mellon alumnus, is known for creating the emoticon ':-)'. He proposed it in 1982 on an electronic board as a way to indicate jokes.
29: Scotty Saturday is a daylong celebration featuring CMU-themed activities, student talent showcases, free snacks, and giveaways.
30: The main administrative hub of Carnegie Mellon University is the David and Susan Coulter Welcome Center. This center serves as the primary hub for campus visits and is located at the front door of the university.
31: The Mobot competition at Carnegie Mellon is an annual event where autonomous robots navigate a slalom course painted on the sidewalk outside Wean Hall. Robots must pass through gates in sequence and reach the finish line without necessarily following the guiding white line.
32: Zachary Quinto, a Carnegie Mellon alumnus, played Spock in the 'Star Trek' reboot films.
33: Carnegie Mellon University is named after Andrew Carnegie and the Mellon family
34: The tradition involving students painting a specific campus landmark at CMU is called "Painting The Fence." Students may paint the Fence between midnight and sunrise using only paintbrushes, and must guard it to keep their message displayed.
35: The Cut refers to a distinctive piece of campus lingo and tradition at Carnegie Mellon University, representing a unique aspect of its student culture. It is part of the school's vernacular that helps shape the campus life experience, alongside other terms like Buggy and Booth. This term encapsulates specific customs or locations significant to CMU students.
36: Luis von Ahn is known for his contributions to the field of artificial intelligence. He is a CMU alumnus and former professor who co-founded Duolingo, an application that uses AI to provide language education to millions of users worldwide.
37: Scotch'n'Soda Theatre is a student-run theatre organization at Carnegie Mellon University that produces original musicals and performs professionally published and student-written materials. Founded in 1907 as The White Friars Club, it is the nation's oldest co-ed student theater group and one of the largest student organizations at CMU.
38: Cohon University Center is named after Andrew W. Cohon, a former CMU president and significant donor, but the building named after the university's founder is Doherty Hall, named after Andrew Carnegie.
39: The Plaid Parliament of Pwning (PPP) is a hacking team at Carnegie Mellon University that competes in Capture the Flag (CTF) cybersecurity competitions. Composed of students from various departments, the team is coached by alumni and faculty and has won multiple titles, including seven victories at the DEF CON CTF.
40: The Carnegie Mellon School of Drama is renowned for its contributions to the arts. This program promotes innovative and self-generated artistic work and has produced numerous talented theater professionals.
41: Mobot is an annual mobile robot competition at Carnegie Mellon University that began in 1994.
42: Andy Bechtolsheim co-founded Sun Microsystems. He is a CMU alumnus who earned his M.S.
43: Sweepstakes, a tradition at Carnegie Mellon University, involves a race with uniquely designed vehicles. Students build and race aerodynamic "buggies" at speeds up to 35 mph during Spring Carnival.
44: The Mall" is a significant grassy area on Carnegie Mellon University's campus that runs perpendicular to "The Cut," serving as a central gathering space for students and community members. It was created as part of the campus design and complements the larger green space that forms the backbone of the campus.
45: Vint Cerf, a notable alumnus of Carnegie Mellon University, is known for his work in computer networking and the development of the ARPANET.
46: Spring Carnival is the oldest tradition at CMU, held annually over three days in April.

50: Deep Thought was a chess computer developed by Carnegie Mellon University in the early 1980s.
51: The NavLab project at CMU was significant because it developed the first series of autonomous vehicles, starting with NavLab 5 which drove autonomously from Pittsburgh to San Diego in 1995. This project demonstrated long-distance autonomous driving capabilities and paved the way for future advancements in self-driving technology.
52: Carnegie Mellon University contributed to the development of speech recognition technology through the creation of SPHINX by Kai-Fu Lee, Raj Reddy, and Roberto Bisiani in 1989. SPHINX was the first real-time speech recognition system using AI techniques. The Language Technologies Institute, founded in 1986, continued to advance speech recognition research and applications.

54: The DARPA Grand Challenge significantly boosted CMU's reputation in robotics and autonomous systems.
55: Carnegie Mellon University played a crucial role in the development of self-driving technology through projects like Navlab 5, which drove autonomously from Pittsburgh to San Diego, and BOSS, a self-driving SUV that won the 2007 DARPA Urban Challenge. CMU continues to contribute to the advancement of self-driving vehicles, influencing their integration into transportation systems, including the concept of using them to help underserved communities.
56: The Iris rover, developed at Carnegie Mellon University, was designed for lunar exploration and played a role in planetary exploration.
57: Libratus is a supercomputer program developed by researchers at Carnegie Mellon University that plays poker.
58: Pittsburgh Restaurant Week is an annual event that celebrates dining in Pittsburgh with winter and summer editions focusing on new dishes and local produce respectively. Restaurants create limited-time menus offering special discounts to attract diners, particularly from surrounding areas.

108: Pittsburgh is named after British statesman William Pitt the Elder
109: Pittsburgh is located in southwestern Pennsylvania
110: Pittsburgh is known by the nickname "The Smoky City" or "The Smoking City" due to the smoke produced by the iron and steel industry.
111: The Pittsburgh region was originally inhabited by several Native American tribes including the Lenape, Shawnee, and the Haudenosaunee (Five Nations). The Lenape were displaced from eastern Pennsylvania by European settlers, while the Shawnee migrated up from the south.
112: The significant event that took place at Fort Duquesne was its capture by the British in November 25, 1758, after the French destroyed and abandoned the fort.
114: In 1758, Pittsburgh was named by Scottish General John Forbes in honor of British statesman William Pitt, 1st Earl of Chatham. This marked the formal naming of the settlement, which had strategic importance during the French and Indian War. The name choice reflected the political alliance and military operations of the time.
115: The Allegheny and Monongahela rivers converge to form the Ohio River in Pittsburgh. This confluence occurs at Point State Park in the city's Golden Triangle area.
116: The Whiskey Rebellion was a violent tax protest against the federal "whiskey tax" imposed in 1791 during George Washington's presidency. In western Pennsylvania, including Pittsburgh, farmers resisted the tax by using violence and intimidation to prevent federal officials from collecting it.
117: Pittsburgh's strategic location at the confluence of the Allegheny and Monongahela rivers, forming the Ohio River, facilitated river transportation and access to raw materials. This geography enabled the establishment of industries like iron and steel, which required waterways for transportation and power. Additionally, the area's abundant coal and iron ore deposits supported the growth of these industries.
118: During the American Civil War, Pittsburgh played a significant role as a major industrial center, supplying arms, ammunition, and other war materials to the Union army. The city's iron and steel industries were crucial for producing military equipment. Additionally, Pittsburgh's strategic location facilitated the movement of troops and supplies along the Ohio and Monongahela rivers.
120: The Fort Pitt Blockhouse is Pittsburgh's oldest architectural landmark, built in 1764 as part of Fort Pitt during the French and Indian War. It is the sole remaining structure of the fort and is owned by the Pittsburgh Chapter of the National Society Daughters of the American Revolution.
121: The H.J. Heinz Company was founded in Pittsburgh in 1869
123: After World War II, Pittsburgh's economy shifted away from heavy industry like steel towards light industry, high-tech sectors, and services. The city implemented redevelopment programs focusing on pollution control and infrastructure improvements. By the 1980s, the steel industry had largely vanished due to foreign competition and reduced demand, leading Pittsburgh to diversify into areas such as computer software, robotics, and biomedical technologies.
124: The Renaissance period in Pittsburgh's history refers to the city's economic and cultural revitalization following its industrial decline. This transformation began in the mid-20th century and involved significant urban renewal projects and diversification of the local economy.
125: The University of Pittsburgh was founded in 1787. It is a state-related school known for its large research program.
126: In 1964, the Pittsburgh Steelers joined the National Football League (NFL), marking the beginning of their professional sports presence in the city. This year laid the foundation for the Steelers' eventual rise to prominence, becoming one of the most successful NFL teams and a cornerstone of Pittsburgh's sports culture.
127: Pittsburgh has 446 bridges, more than any other city in the world. This includes 10 bridges in Downtown and 18 in an expanded definition of Downtown that includes surrounding neighborhoods.
128: Point State Park is a 36-acre park located at the tip of Pittsburgh's Golden Triangle, where the Allegheny and Monongahela Rivers meet to form the Ohio River. The park features expansive lawns, riverfront trails, and the famous Point State Park Fountain, which sprays water up to 150 feet into the air.
129: Oakland is known for its cultural diversity, featuring institutions like the Carnegie Museums and the Latin American Cultural Center. This neighborhood is home to several universities and a variety of cultural attractions.
130: The Cathedral of Learning is a 42-story, 535-foot-tall Late Gothic Revival skyscraper that serves as the centerpiece of the University of Pittsburgh's main campus in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. It is the tallest educational building in the Western Hemisphere and houses various academic departments, including the Dietrich School of Arts and Sciences and the School of Social Work.
131: Alcoa is the Pittsburgh-based company known for its aluminum products. Founded in Pittsburgh, it helped establish the nation's aluminum industry in the early 20th century.
132: The Homestead Strike was a significant labor confrontation in 1892 that lasted 143 days, centered at Carnegie Steel's Homestead plant in Pennsylvania. It grew out of a dispute between the Amalgamated Association of Iron and Steel Workers and the Carnegie Steel Company over wages and working conditions. Despite the strike's violent outcome, it contributed to the development of modern labor practices and laws protecting workers.
133: Andy Warhol was born in the neighborhood of Oakland in Pittsburgh. Oakland is known for its educational and cultural institutions, including the Carnegie Museums.
134: The Pittsburgh Renaissance Historic District is not directly defined in the given context.
135: Carnegie Museum of Art is dedicated to contemporary art, being one of the first museums of contemporary art in the United States. It features over 34,000 works emphasizing art, architecture, photography, and design from the 19th century to the present.
136: In 1979, the Pittsburgh Steelers won Super Bowl XIII, marking one of their six Super Bowl victories. Willie Stargell also won the BBWAA Award that year, sharing it with another player. This year highlighted Pittsburgh's success in major sports with both the Steelers and Pirates achieving significant milestones.
137: Downtown Pittsburgh is known for its historic architecture
138: Phipps Conservatory and Botanical Gardens is a botanical garden located in Schenley Park, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
139: Acrisure Stadium is home to the Pittsburgh Steelers. Located in the North Shore neighborhood of Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, it opened in 2001.
140: The Carnegie Science Center is one of the four dynamic museums established by Andrew Carnegie in 1895, part of Carnegie Museums of Pittsburgh. It focuses on connecting science and technology with everyday life through exhibits and educational programs.
141: Andrew Carnegie was a Scottish-American industrialist and philanthropist who founded the world's largest steel company and donated $1 million to establish the Carnegie Technical Schools in Pittsburgh in 1900, later evolving into Carnegie Mellon University. His impact on Pittsburgh included providing educational opportunities for working-class individuals and significant contributions to the city's economic development through his steel industry.
142: Lawrenceville is known for its vibrant arts scene, featuring artists and designers. It hosts various galleries, studios, and events that celebrate local creativity.
143: Unique to Pittsburgh's Inclines is their role in connecting residents and visitors between the bottom and top of Mount Washington, offering iconic views of the city skyline. The Duquesne Incline features red cars visible from The Point, while the Monongahela Incline has yellow cars visible from the South Shore.
144: The Nationality Rooms program at the University of Pittsburgh involves rooms within the Cathedral of Learning, each designed by different cultural groups that significantly contributed to Pittsburgh's development before 1787. These rooms celebrate various cultures through authentic artifacts and designs funded and created by community committees, with the university providing only the space and subsequent maintenance.
145: The longest bridge in Pittsburgh is the Fort Pitt Bridge, also known as the West End Bridge, which spans approximately 3,461 feet across the Ohio River.
146: The Smithfield Street Bridge is significant as it is the oldest river crossing in Pittsburgh, opening in 1883, and it was the world's first lenticular truss bridge. Designated as both a Historic Civil Engineering Landmark and a National Historic Landmark, it played a crucial role in establishing Pittsburgh as the "City of Bridges.
147: The famous sandwiches associated with Pittsburgh are the Primanti Brothers sandwiches, which feature grilled meat, cheese, coleslaw, tomatoes, and French fries served between two slices of Italian bread.
148: The Allegheny County Courthouse serves as a judicial facility where county court proceedings take place.

151: Carnegie Mellon University is known for robotics research. The Robotics Institute at Carnegie Mellon University was established in 1979 and continues to be a leader in the field.
152: The Golden Triangle is the downtown business district of Pittsburgh, located at the confluence of the Allegheny and Monongahela rivers, where they form the Ohio River. Bounded by these rivers, it is also officially known as the Central Business District and is the heart of Pittsburgh's corporate and financial activities.
153: The oldest operating theater in Pittsburgh is the Byham Theater
154: The Carnegie Museums are located in the Oakland neighborhood of Pittsburgh. Specifically, the Carnegie Museum of Art and the Carnegie Museum of Natural History are part of the Carnegie Institute and Library complex in Oakland.
155: The Pittsburgh Zoo & Aquarium is known for offering year-round fun with exciting animal exhibits and educational experiences. It features a variety of animals and has been a Pittsburgh staple for 125 years.
156: The Roberto Clemente Bridge, originally known as the Sixth Street Bridge, has been rebuilt three times since 1819. Its current structure, a self-anchored suspension bridge, was built in 1928 and renamed in honor of Pittsburgh Pirates baseball player Roberto Clemente on August 6, 1998.
